draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond better to encouraging, conversational tone over neutral weather data delivery
facade.label: Motivational voice
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into celebratory language, cheer-like notifications, personal address ('your pace', 'crush it')
facade.rejectImplies: Keep tone neutral and data-focused; minimize personality and cheerleading in weather/run feedback

evidence.hypothesis: Runners respond better to encouraging, conversational tone over neutral weather data delivery
evidence.implication: Lean into celebratory language, cheer-like notifications, personal address ('your pace', 'crush it')

facade.hypothesis: Runner-specific data (pace, HR, route) works best in a persistent left panel while navigation tabs stay anchored at bottom
facade.label: Sidebar with tabs below
facade.acceptImplies: Users want quick glances at live metrics without losing nav context; sidebar reduces cognitive load during runs
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer full-screen focused views or mobile-first tab-only navigation; sidebars feel desktop-heavy for active use

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond better to celebratory micro-motions when they hit workout milestones, creating a sense of achievement
facade.axisTargeted: Gesture reward feedback—subtle celebratory animation vs silent confirmation
facade.label: Bounce on landing
facade.acceptImplies: Explore feedback patterns that use motion to reinforce accomplishment moments; runners need emotional payoff, not just data
facade.rejectImplies: Focus on understated, minimal feedback; runners want crisp clarity over celebration; motion should be invisible or functional only

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond better to high-contrast, glowing call-to-action buttons (bright orange, lime, or neon) over subtle or muted controls.
facade.label: Bright action buttons
facade.acceptImplies: The app should emphasize interactive elements with vibrant, eye-catching colors to drive quick decisions mid-run.
facade.rejectImplies: The app should use restrained, minimal button styling that blends with the interface—action feels calm and integrated, not urgent.

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer either precise metrics (pace, HR, distance) or narrative context (how they feel, effort zone, story). Information design choice between quantitative density vs. qualitative framing.
facade.label: Numbers or words?
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into numeric precision: split times, heart rate zones, mileage breakdowns. Design with data density and scannable figures.
facade.rejectImplies: Lean into prose and sensation: 'easy effort', 'pushing hard', emoji mood, effort descriptions. Hide raw numbers or make them secondary.

facade.hypothesis: A dark, high-contrast running app with sparse UI reduces cognitive load during workouts and feels focused/technical rather than playful
facade.axisTargeted: Digital–Organic (leaning cold/minimal vs warm/natural) and Calm–Energetic (restraint vs stimulation)
facade.label: Minimalist dark interface
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward stripped-down, performance-focused design; values efficiency and a 'serious athlete' vibe over encouragement or personality
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers either warmer tones, more whitespace/light backgrounds, or visual elements that feel encouraging/human rather than austere

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive menu navigation for weather info during runs
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, zen atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented mood for pre-run prep
Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, UV, wind gusts)
Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback when checking conditions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive menu navigation for weather info during runs
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, zen atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented mood for pre-run prep
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, UV, wind gusts)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback when checking conditions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners in training mode prefer a no-nonsense, data-first voice focused on conditions and performance metrics over motivational framing
facade.axisTargeted: voice and personality — professional vs friendly
facade.label: All business tone
facade.acceptImplies: Double down on factual, efficient language; minimize celebratory language; prioritize clarity and practical info
facade.rejectImplies: Motivational tone resonates more than purely functional delivery; consider blending encouragement with weather facts

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.

Good for Running

Mild temps, light wind. Best window: next 2 hours.
